caption: Last night someone asked me what part of Dear New York I’m proudest of; this was hard to answer. But I can tell you what’s filled my heart the most. Many in the press are calling it ‘The Children’s Exhibit,’ because Dear New York’s Vanderbilt Hall features the work of six hundred NYC student artists. Each student was asked to photograph and write about someone who has made a difference in their lives. Some of these artists are as young as four, and their photography might not be the most technically polished, but there is a striking intimacy that can only come from photographing someone you love. The written profiles which accompany the photographs are extremely touching, and collectively represent the millions of invisible, unsung heroes that form the bedrock of NYC’s community. It’s not the mayors and the movie stars that form the heart of this city. It’s the mothers, the fathers, the teachers, the social workers, and the millions of other people who show up every day in the lives of others. One reason the ‘Children’s Exhibit’ lands so deeply is that its amplified by a sister exhibition featuring eleven highly notable NYC photographers. Among them are living legends such as @jamelshabazz , a trailblazer in street portraiture. As well as Gugenheim Fellow @sarabennettbrooklyn , a former public defender who profiles formerly-incarcerated women. The two exhibits are blended seamlessly in an innovative, immersive design by @davidkorins . The most beautiful part of these blended exhibits is that all 611 artists— young and old, beginning and established— have been given the same museum-quality framing and presentation. The result is an egalitarian, non-hierarchical ode to the diversity, humanity, and community of this city.  The exhibit has been overflowing all week, from open to close, and I’m not sure I’ve ever seen an art installation elicit so much emotion. Dear New York closes on Sunday. For everyone who attends the final Sunday, you will be able to meet all eleven featured professional artists, who will be there to greet visitors between 2 pm and 5 pm.

Still feeling the positive effect today and it will linger in my heart ❤️ thanks to Brandon and to the whole team for this remarkable exhibition!! A true love letter to NY- and the young people choral group’s medley was pure perfection!!
I wish I could go see this exhibit! So exceptional.
Gotta ❤️ New York City 🌺

The whole thing was special,but the children's exhibit really made me cry. I got to see one of the children who was selected, with her whole family, look at her artwork and celebrate. That was even more special.

caption: “Her name is Rita. It comes from a poem by a well-known poet of Palestine. It’s a song that I used to sing, and my wife loves it too. So Rita is a product of love between me and my wife. I haven’t seen either of them in a year and a half. When the border opened last year, I sent them both out of the country. I hold a Master’s degree in Burns, Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery—and these skills are deeply needed in Gaza. So I chose to stay behind. Now the border is closed, and I couldn’t leave if I wanted to. I do my best to talk to Rita on WhatsApp every day. I’ll ask about kindergarten, and her swimming lessons. I’ll look at her latest drawings, and try to encourage her. But that’s it; I’m a WhatsApp father only. A few days ago she was ignoring me a bit, so I asked: ‘Why don’t you talk to me?’ She told me: ‘I feel like I forgot you a little bit.’ My wife jumped in, and said: ‘No, no, she’s kidding.’ But I said: ‘She doesn’t need to be kidding. She’s saying the truth.’ I’ve been gone most of her life. First with my studies in England, and now with this separation. I do feel guilty, yes. But if I had left, I’d feel even more guilty. There’d be a crack in my heart. It would come to me in my nightmares, every time I saw the suffering of my people. The first stage of this genocide was to destroy the health infrastructure, so there’d be no refuge for the dying. I happen to be part of an organization, Doctors Without Borders, with big abilities and big principles. I knew that if we worked together, and made the right decisions, and did the right things—we’d be able to provide comfort to countless people. And that’s what we’ve done. I’ve co-managed a team that built a field hospital with 120 beds, and three surgical theaters running 24 hours. This is something I’ll be proud of for years and years. And I hope one day Rita will understand. I’ll ask her to read books and watch documentaries about the genocide. I’ll ask her to understand that I couldn’t close my eyes to the suffering of our people. We must resist. And how do we resist? By existing. And how do we exist? By having a value. And in our family, our value is to serve others, more than we serve ourselves.”
